text: The surgeon opens the pararectal spaces on both sides, creating a large pararectal window to safely perform adhesiolysis. The dissection targets the para rectal and para sigmoidal fat to protect the bowel lumen. The anterior lateral aspect of the rectum is exposed, revealing the coiled sigmoid and nodular growth impinging on the posterior.

The surgeon uses a Bissinger bipolar instrument to grasp tissues and apply gentle counter traction. The nodule, identified by its stone-like consistency, is being cut through to expose it fully for dissection. Traction is applied to the uterus in a cranial direction using a needle holder, aiding in the dissection of the stony hard nodule that causes the rectum to coil due to nodularity in the pararectal margins.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the anterior aspect of the rectum, ensuring adequate rectal length from the anorectal junction for future anastomosis. The fibrosed nodule causes bowel dilatation above it, and dissection proceeds below the nodule from the Denonvilliers fascia. Suction is used to separate the nodule from its posterior vaginal attachment, and the Bissinger bipolar exposes the pararectal fossa. A native tissue plane is obtained, allowing separation of the rectum's anterolateral and anterior aspects up to the perineal body. Similar dissection is performed on the opposite side.

The surgeon approaches the anterior aspect of the rectum by dissecting through the anterolateral plane. A bold cut is made through the anterior margin of the rectum, releasing the bowel from the fibrotic cluster caused by the pararectal nodule. The dissection reveals a large nodularity and puckering of the bowel, indicating unhealthy tissue. The bowel is dissected through its length to the perineal body, highlighting the need for bowel resection due to luminal constriction and stricture.

The surgeon prepares the bowel for resection and anastomosis by exposing the pararectal fat, ensuring minimal exposure to maintain vascularity. Using a laparoscopic shearer, the surgeon performs circumferential dissection to reveal the lumen of the healthy rectum, indicating readiness for anastomosis.

The surgeon prepares the distal stump of the bowel and passes the linear endo GI stapler, ensuring the bowel is correctly positioned within the stapler blades. The port is widened to accommodate the stapler, and the surgeon checks for any nearby structures, such as the ureter, before firing the stapler to achieve a clean cut through the bowel.

The surgeon fires the linear endo GI stapler, creating a three-row cut through the distal stump of the bowel. After removing the stapler, scissors are used to refashion the stump. The proximal stump is prepared laparoscopically, with a bowel clamp inserted through a 5mm port, followed by cutting with scissors. A small suprapubic incision is made to insert the anvil for the circular stapler, and a purse string suture is tied using polyglactin to secure the bowel, ensuring a snug fit for the anvil inside the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon passes the circular stapler through the anus, positioning it on the anterior aspect of the distal stump for an end-to-side anastomosis. The circular stapler pin is visible entering the long distal stump. The anvil is fitted onto the opposite end of the stapler, and the orange mark on the stapler indicates readiness to fire. The surgeon verifies the alignment and vascularization of the anastomotic site before firing the stapler.

The surgeon prepares to fire the circular stapler, ensuring the anti-mycentric borders of the colon are aligned to prevent twisting. The stapler is fired and held for ten seconds before being gently removed through the anal canal. The uterus remains intact, indicating a conservative approach. The staple line is visible, and the anvil with both donuts confirms a successful end-to-side anastomosis.
